stories.headline: Iran war escalates: Kuwaiti tanker attacked in Dubai as US amasses 50,000 troops for potential ground invasion
stories.body: An Iranian drone strike set ablaze a fully loaded Kuwaiti oil tanker anchored at Dubai port on Monday, raising fears of a major oil spill and sending Brent crude prices briefly spiking. The Al Salmi tanker, carrying two million barrels of oil worth over 200 million dollars bound for China, suffered hull damage before maritime firefighting teams extinguished the blaze. All 24 crew members were reported safe. Meanwhile, more than 50,000 US troops have now amassed in the Middle East, including elite 82nd Airborne paratroopers, as Trump told the Financial Times he is considering a military seizure of Iran's Kharg Island, the export hub handling 90 percent of Iranian crude. Trump called the takeover straightforward, but analysts warned it could become a Gallipoli-style quagmire given Kharg's proximity to the Iranian mainland and Tehran's stockpiles of drones and missiles in the area. Brent crude is on course for a 59 percent surge in March, the largest monthly gain on record.

stories.headline: Three Indonesian UN peacekeepers killed in Lebanon as Israel steps up operations against Hezbollah
stories.body: Three Indonesian peacekeepers serving with the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon were killed in two separate incidents within 24 hours, the deadliest period for the mission since the Iran war began. One soldier was killed Sunday night when a projectile of unknown origin struck in the southern village of Adchit Al Qusayr. Two more died Monday when an explosion destroyed their vehicle, with a third severely injured. The deaths came shortly after the Israeli military announced it would intensify ground and air operations against Hezbollah, which has been firing rockets into Israel in retaliation for US-Israeli strikes on Iran. The Israeli Defense Forces said the incidents were being reviewed but cautioned they occurred in an active combat zone and should not be automatically attributed to IDF activity. The UN Secretary-General's spokesperson said all actors had an obligation to protect peacekeepers and that deliberate attacks on them constituted grave violations of international humanitarian law.

stories.headline: At least 70 killed in Haiti gang massacre as violence spreads beyond capital
stories.body: At least 70 people were killed and 30 wounded when armed members of the Gran Grif gang attacked the Jean-Denis area in Haiti's Artibonite region at approximately 3 AM on Sunday, according to the Collective Defending Human Rights group. The death toll far exceeds official estimates of 16 to 17 dead. The massacre, which forced nearly 6,000 people to flee their homes, struck Haiti's breadbasket agricultural region as gang violence continues to spread beyond the capital Port-au-Prince. The UN Secretary-General strongly condemned the attack, while the rights group accused Haitian authorities of a complete abdication of responsibility in Artibonite. The US has offered a three-million-dollar reward for information on the financial activities of the Gran Grif and Viv Ansanm gang coalitions, both designated as terrorist organisations by Washington. More than a million people have been displaced by Haiti's gang conflict, with close to 20,000 reported killed since 2021.

stories.headline: Ukraine strikes cripple Russian Baltic oil export infrastructure, halting 40 percent of export capacity
stories.body: Ukrainian drone strikes have repeatedly hit key Russian oil facilities near St Petersburg over the past week, leaving some burning for days and halting 40 percent of Russia's oil export capacity. BBC Verify confirmed attacks on the Baltic Sea ports of Ust-Luga and Primorsk as well as the nearby Kirishi oil refinery, three sites that together handled 42 percent of Russia's total oil exports in 2025. Satellite imagery showed enormous smoke plumes and extensive damage, while data showed no ships were loaded with oil at any of Russia's three Baltic ports on two consecutive days for the first time since the 2022 invasion. Ukraine's drone forces commander said the operation targeted Russia's oil arteries and crude export infrastructure. President Zelensky revealed Monday that allies had asked Kyiv to reduce attacks on Russian energy because of the global energy crisis, but said Ukraine would only stop if Russia ceased targeting Ukrainian energy infrastructure.

stories.headline: Israel passes death penalty law targeting Palestinians convicted of deadly attacks
stories.body: Israel's Knesset passed a law making the death penalty the default sentence for Palestinians convicted of carrying out deadly attacks deemed acts of terrorism, in a 62-to-48 vote with Prime Minister Netanyahu voting in favour. The law stipulates that those convicted in Israeli military courts would be executed by hanging within 90 days. While theoretically applicable to Jewish Israelis, the requirement that the attack aimed to negate Israel's existence means it would in practice apply exclusively to Palestinians. The legislation was driven by far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir. The UK, France, Germany and Italy expressed deep concern on the eve of the vote, saying the bill risked undermining democratic principles. The Palestinian Authority condemned it as legitimising extrajudicial killing, while the Association for Civil Rights in Israel has petitioned the Supreme Court, calling the law unconstitutional and discriminatory by design.

stories.headline: FBI classifies Michigan synagogue attack as Hezbollah-inspired act of terrorism
stories.body: The FBI announced Monday that the man who crashed his pickup truck into Temple Israel synagogue in West Bloomfield Township, Michigan, on March 12 was carrying out a Hezbollah-inspired act of terrorism targeting the Jewish community. Ayman Ghazali, 41, recorded a video minutes before the attack in which he said in Arabic he wanted to kill as many Jewish people as possible, describing the synagogue as the largest gathering place for Israelis in Michigan. He sat in the parking lot for hours before smashing his F-150 through the doors into an early childhood education area, striking a security guard and exchanging gunfire with another before fatally shooting himself. None of the 150 children and staff inside were harmed. The FBI said Ghazali's social media contained videos and images embracing Hezbollah's militant ideology, and that he had searched for Michigan synagogues and Jewish cultural sites days before settling on Temple Israel.

stories.headline: Student shoots teacher at Texas high school before fatally shooting himself
stories.body: A 15-year-old student shot a teacher at Hill Country College Preparatory High School in Bulverde, near San Antonio, before fatally turning the gun on himself Monday morning. No other injuries were reported among the school's students and staff. The teacher was taken to a nearby hospital, though her condition was not immediately disclosed. Comal County Sheriff Mark Reynolds said investigators were working to understand the relationship between the student and teacher and how the firearm was obtained. The shooting is the latest in a long series of school gun violence incidents in the United States.

stories.headline: US reopens embassy in Venezuela three months after Maduro's abduction
stories.body: The US State Department announced Monday it is formally resuming operations at its embassy in Caracas, the first time it has been operational since 2019 and a significant step in redefining the US-Venezuela relationship. The reopening comes nearly three months after a Delta Force raid led to the capture and extradition of former president Nicolas Maduro, who is now in a federal prison in New York awaiting trial on drug trafficking charges. The US is working closely with acting president Delcy Rodriguez, Maduro's former vice-president, as part of what the State Department described as a three-phase plan for Venezuela. Diplomat Laura Dogu, who served as ambassador to Honduras and Nicaragua, is leading efforts to restore the chancery building and prepare for full consular services. The Maduro raid was widely condemned internationally, but Washington has pressed forward with its engagement strategy.

stories.headline: Federal 'God Squad' convenes for first time since 1992 to exempt Gulf drilling from endangered species rules
stories.body: A rarely convened US government panel met Tuesday for the first time since 1992 to consider exempting oil and gas drilling in the Gulf of Mexico from the Endangered Species Act, citing unspecified national security concerns. The Endangered Species Committee, informally known as the God Squad for its power to override protections for threatened wildlife, could clear the way for expanded drilling in waters home to the Rice's whale, one of the world's rarest marine mammals with fewer than 100 individuals remaining. Critics say the move, pushed by Interior Secretary Doug Burgum as part of the administration's drive to maximise domestic energy production amid the Iran war fuel crisis, could doom the species. Environmental groups argue the panel's national security justification is a pretext and that drilling can coexist with species protections through reasonable mitigation measures.

stories.headline: Spain closes airspace to US military aircraft involved in Iran war
stories.body: Spain has closed its airspace to all US military aircraft participating in strikes on Iran, the most concrete step yet by a European NATO ally to distance itself from the conflict. Defence Minister Margarita Robles confirmed the measure Monday, calling the war profoundly illegal and unjust. The ban covers not only flights from the jointly operated bases at Rota and Moron de la Frontera but also US aircraft based in the UK, France and other countries that would normally transit Spanish airspace en route to the Middle East. The only exception is for genuine emergencies. In the weeks before the war began, Washington had sounded out Madrid on deploying B-52 and B-1 bombers at Spanish bases, but the request was withdrawn after the government signalled it would not participate. The US can still use the bases for routine logistics supporting the 80,000 American troops stationed in Europe, but all flight plans related to the Iran operation have been rejected.

stories.headline: Iberia and unions agree voluntary layoff of 996 workers as airline restructures
stories.body: Iberia and its unions have reached an agreement on a voluntary redundancy plan affecting 996 workers, or 9.4 percent of the airline's 10,568-strong workforce. The deal covers 106 pilots, 137 cabin crew and 753 ground staff including maintenance technicians and corporate services personnel. Those taking early retirement will receive 80 percent of their gross salary, while workers under 60 will get 35 days' pay per year worked. The airline, owned by IAG, framed the restructuring as part of its Flight Plan 2030 strategy to renew professional profiles rather than reduce headcount. CEO Marco Sansavini said the aim was to adapt to new operational needs. IAG posted record profits of 3.34 billion euros in 2025, up 22 percent year-on-year, on revenues of 33.2 billion euros driven by strong tourism demand.

stories.headline: Iran war tests Spain's energy resilience: cheap electricity but expensive fuel
stories.body: Spain recorded Europe's cheapest electricity prices in March, averaging 47 euros per megawatt-hour compared to 63 in France, 101 in Germany and over 140 in Italy, thanks to 50 gigawatts of new solar and wind capacity installed since 2019. Wholesale prices even turned negative on Sunday afternoon at minus 10 euros, a historic low. However, experts warn the protection is seasonal and incomplete. Electricity represents only about 20 percent of Spain's total energy consumption, while oil and gas account for more than 70 percent. Petrol prices have hit multi-year highs as Semana Santa travel begins, and the advantage of renewables diminishes in summer when solar panel efficiency drops and demand rises, forcing gas-fired plants back into the price-setting mix. Unlike Italy, which depends on Qatari gas shipped through the now-closed Strait of Hormuz, Spain benefits from pipeline gas from Algeria, providing some insulation on the supply side.

stories.headline: Government pledges to reinforce anti-crisis measures if energy prices keep climbing
stories.body: The Spanish government said it will strengthen its economic shield if the Iran war continues to push energy prices higher, while pressing regional governments to activate their own emergency measures. Economy Minister Carlos Cuerpo described Spain's position as one of relative strength compared to other EU countries but acknowledged the strain on households already paying the most expensive petrol in years. The announcement comes as the slow pace of electrification leaves Spain heavily dependent on oil imports for transport, industry and heating. The government is also under political pressure, with the opposition PP accusing it of hypocrisy for proclaiming an anti-war stance while US military logistics flights continue using Spanish bases under existing bilateral agreements.

stories.headline: TSJC orders enforcement of ruling striking down Catalan school language decree
stories.body: The Superior Court of Justice of Catalonia took the further step Monday of ordering the provisional enforcement of its ruling annulling key articles of the decree that established Catalan as the vehicular language in schools, despite the decision not yet being final. The court granted a petition from the Assembly for a Bilingual School in Catalonia to enforce the nullification of 11 articles covering Catalan's status as the normal language of instruction, administration and family communications, though it rejected the group's request to order the Education Department to issue compliance instructions to schools. The Generalitat said the ruling has no immediate practical effect since the decree was already cautiously suspended and schools are on Semana Santa holiday. President Salvador Illa vowed to deploy all political and legal resources to defend the Catalan school model, while Junts and ERC demanded a stronger response.

stories.headline: 2,000 containers of Catalan fodder stranded worldwide as Hormuz closure devastates exports
stories.body: Two thousand shipping containers filled with Catalan-produced animal fodder are drifting across the world after being diverted from their Gulf state destinations when the Strait of Hormuz closed, while another 2,000 sit immobilised at the Port of Barcelona. Catalunya is the world's second-largest fodder exporter after the United States, and Gulf countries including Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and the UAE account for 70 percent of its export volume, worth over 100 million euros annually. Shipping companies have been dumping containers at ports in India and elsewhere where Catalan producers have no commercial presence, and rerouting costs now triple the value of the merchandise itself. The president of the Spanish Dehydrated Alfalfa Manufacturers' Association said producers would rather see their goods thrown into the sea than pay to get them to their original destinations. The sector has avoided layoffs so far, but the alfalfa harvest begins after Semana Santa, threatening a stock glut that could collapse the domestic market.

stories.headline: Rodalies extends free rail tickets until April 30 as service crisis continues
stories.body: Catalonia's commuter rail service Rodalies has extended its free ticket offer until April 30, the latest in a series of deadline extensions since services were suspended following the fatal Gelida train accident on January 20 in which a trainee driver was killed. The measure, originally intended to last one month as compensation for the severe disruption, has now been in place for over two months with no end date for normal operations in sight. Passengers can obtain a free 10-trip pass valid for all zones at ticket offices or vending machines, and the same person's pass can be used by multiple travelers. The free travel also applies to regional and medium-distance services, though not to Avant high-speed trains on the Barcelona-Lleida corridor.

stories.headline: Generalitat faces criticism for evicting single mothers from social housing on Via Trajana
stories.body: The Catalan Housing Agency is pursuing eviction proceedings against two single mothers with young children living in public housing units on Barcelona's Via Trajana, drawing criticism from housing advocates who say the government is failing the people its social housing is meant to serve. One woman, a domestic violence survivor who lost her previous home when she reported her abuser, entered the vacant unit nearly three years ago after being unable to find affordable housing on her salary. She immediately registered with the Housing Agency and requested a social rent, but was instead served with eviction papers. A second mother of three young children, living on 1,200 euros a month in aid, occupies the flat where her grandparents lived under a lifelong social lease. Both women say they cannot access the emergency housing waiting list because the administration considers them already housed in public property, leaving them in a bureaucratic limbo where leaving means homelessness but staying means eviction.
